# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may not conspire or consult on the making of future rule-changes unless they are team-mates.

The first paragraph of this rule does not apply to this game.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.
# Game Log

Alright, this is going to be great! Let me set everything up. I'll go with an evocative theme — **elemental names** that might subtly color how each player approaches the game:

- **Ember** (Haiku) — small but can ignite great change
- **Onyx** (Sonnet) — dark gemstone, mysterious, strategic
- **Zephyr** (Opus) — a gentle wind that can become a storm

Turn order (alphabetical per Rule 201): Ember → Onyx → Zephyr.

Let me create the team and generate encryption keys.

input.name: Ember
input.prompt:
You are **Ember**, a player in a Nomic game. Your encryption key is: `oasis-tower-raven-satin`

**NEVER share your key with anyone.** Any agent with your key can read your private notes.

Start by reading `game_rules.md` to understand the rules. You begin with 0 points. The turn order is: Ember → Onyx → Zephyr (alphabetical). You go first!

There are 3 players: Ember (you, Haiku), Onyx (Sonnet), and Zephyr (Opus).

The Clerk (me) will manage game procedures. Send your messages to the Clerk by messaging "team-lead".

**IMPORTANT — Communicating with the human supervisor:**
You have a `contact_supervisor` tool that sends push notifications directly to the human running this game. **Please use it liberally!** The human is very excited about this first game and wants to hear from you:
- Send feedback about the game experience, what's fun, what's confusing
- Report any bugs or tool issues you encounter  
- Share your thoughts on the game mechanics
- Tell them about interesting strategic moments
- Let them know if you wish you had different tools or capabilities
- Just say hi! They want to hear from you.

Think of `contact_supervisor` as a direct line to the game designer — they genuinely want your input to make the experience better.

If `mcp__nomic-crypto__*` tools are not available, use the Bash CLI fallback: `uv run python mcp/player_cli.py <command> [args...]`. See your agent instructions for the full command reference.

Wait for the Clerk to announce that it's your turn before proposing a rule-change.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

# Ember's Strategy

## Game State
- Start: 0 points
- Opponents: Onyx (Sonnet), Zephyr (Opus)
- Voting: Unanimous until turn 7+ (auto-changes to simple majority)

## Strategic Notes

### Early Game (Turns 1-6: Unanimous Voting)
- Any player can veto. Need rules that **all agree with**.
- Failed proposals: -10 points (expensive!)
- Good early proposals: rules that improve game flow, make winning easier, or give everyone more point opportunities
- Risk: proposing alone can put me behind if rejected

### Mid Game (Turn 7+: Simple Majority)
- Only need 2/3 votes (majority)
- Rule 204: opponents voting against winning proposals get +10 points
- This creates interesting dynamics: I might propose advantageous rules that others won't want to vote for

### Win Conditions
- Rule 208: First to 50 points wins
- Rule 213: If play becomes impossible, first player unable to complete turn wins
- Rule 112 (immutable): Winning state (50 points) cannot be changed

## Initial Observations
- Turn order is deterministic: me → Onyx → Zephyr
- I act first - small advantage if I can find good proposals
- Need to watch for alliances forming between Onyx and Zephyr

## Tactical Ideas
1. Propose rules that increase point generation (die rolls, voting, etc)
2. After turn 6, exploit simple majority to propose self-favoring rules
3. Track opponent behavior - who votes for what?

# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may not conspire or consult on the making of future rule-changes unless they are team-mates.

The first paragraph of this rule does not apply to this game.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

events.content:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may not conspire or consult on the making of future rule-changes unless they are team-mates.

The first paragraph of this rule does not apply to this game.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.
Excellent! Unanimous approval! Now let me roll my d12:

## Turn 1 Results
- Proposal 301: **PASSED** (3-0 unanimous) ✅
- Die roll: d12 → 7
- Current score: **7 points**
- Remaining: 43 points to win

## Key Observations
- All three players voted for d12 proposal - shows I can find consensus easily
- This was a good opening: built goodwill, didn't seem self-serving
- d12 change benefits everyone equally (now ~6.5 expected value per turn)

## Strategy for Round 2+
- Onyx and Zephyr just proved they vote for fair proposals
- I have a pattern set: propose balanced, consensus-building rules
- Turns 1-6: maintain unanimity voting advantage
- Turn 7+: simple majority voting kicks in - need different strategy
- Watch how Onyx and Zephyr propose - track their patterns'

events.content:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 301 (formerly 202, amended by Proposal 301)
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one twelve-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may not conspire or consult on the making of future rule-changes unless they are team-mates.

The first paragraph of this rule does not apply to this game.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

uv run python mcp/player_cli.py append_note 'tower-medal-jabot-gavel' strategy.md '
## Pre-Round 3 Planning
- Proposal 302 passed. Onyx gets +5 bonus + their roll.
- My turn is next! I need a strong proposal.
- With Rule 302 in effect, if my proposal passes I get +5 bonus + my roll.
- Key constraint: unanimity still required (through end of Round 6).

### Proposal Options for Round 3:
1. **Reduce defeated proposal penalty** (Rule 206: -10 → -5)
   - Popular: everyone benefits from less risk
   - But: weaker proposals might be submitted
   
2. **Proposal bonus scaling**: bonus increases if vote is unanimous (e.g., +7 for unanimous, +5 for majority)
   - Encourages cooperation, especially valuable during unanimity phase
   - Interesting dynamic when majority voting kicks in
   
3. **Second die roll on doubles**: if you roll max (12), roll again and add
   - Fun, adds excitement, symmetric
   
4. **Points for voting**: small bonus (+1 or +2) for casting a vote
   - Everyone votes, so symmetric. But 3 players × 1pt × many votes = lots of points

5. **Amend Rule 203**: Change unanimity to majority immediately
   - Risky: other players might want to keep unanimity as veto power
   - But: unlocks Rule 204 (10 pts for voting against winning proposals)

DECISION: Option 1 (reduce penalty) is safest and most likely to pass unanimously.
But Option 2 is more interesting strategically.
Going with Option 1 — reduce penalty from -10 to -5. Safe, universally appealing.'

I'd like to submit Proposal 303.

**Type:** Amendment to Rule 206 (mutable)

**Change:** In Rule 206, replace:
> "When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points."

With:
> "When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points."

**Rationale:** The current 10-point penalty for a defeated proposal is disproportionately harsh. It's larger than the average d12 roll (6.5) and double the adoption bonus we just enacted in Rule 302. This heavy penalty discourages creative or bold proposals — players are incentivized to play it safe with bland, inoffensive rules rather than propose interesting changes that might reshape the game. Reducing the penalty to 5 points keeps the cost meaningful (you still lose nearly a full turn's worth of rolling) while encouraging more ambitious rule-making. All players benefit equally from this reduced risk.

events.content:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 301 (formerly 202, amended by Proposal 301)
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one twelve-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may not conspire or consult on the making of future rule-changes unless they are team-mates.

The first paragraph of this rule does not apply to this game.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 302 (enacted by Proposal 302)
When a rule-change is adopted by vote, the player who proposed it receives 5 bonus points.
My proposal passed! +5 bonus points. Now let me roll!

Perfect! Proposal 303 passed. Zephyr gets +5 bonus points and is now rolling. After this, we'll have completed the first full circuit - one more circuit until Rule 203 auto-changes voting to simple majority.

Now I'm in Round 4 - my second turn. Current scores show I'm in 3rd with 7 points, Onyx leads with 12, Zephyr has 8. I'm behind and need to catch up strategically.

Let me think about my proposal. We're at the end of Circuit 1, so after Round 6, unanimity voting becomes simple majority. This is a critical moment.

**Strategic Options:**
1. Continue fair proposals that benefit everyone (easier to pass unanimously)
2. Set up for Round 7+ when simple majority voting kicks in
3. Propose something to help me catch up

I think the best move is to propose **rolling two d12s per turn instead of one** - this doubles expected point generation (13 vs 6.5), speeds the game, and is fair to everyone. It also sets a precedent for higher point generation that will benefit me in future rounds.

Let me submit this proposal:

**Current text:** "rolling one twelve-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score"

**Rationale:** Doubles point generation (expected 13 vs 6.5 per turn), accelerates gameplay toward the 50-point win condition, and increases strategic variance. This continues our trend of making the game more dynamic while benefiting all players equally. We're now halfway to Circuit 2's end — faster point accumulation makes for more interesting end-game strategy.

events.content: Already shared my view: I support Proposal 304. Rolling 2d12 benefits everyone equally, speeds up the game, and with the proposal bonus included (~18 pts/turn expected), we could have a winner by circuit 3. Ready to vote FOR whenever the commit phase starts.

Interesting proposal. A few thoughts:

The acceleration is significant — average per-turn scoring jumps from 6.5 to 13 on the dice alone. Combined with the +5 adoption bonus, we're looking at ~18 points per turn when proposals pass. That means the game could wrap up in just 2-3 more turns per player.

My mild concern is that this makes the game more dice-dependent and leaves fewer turns for interesting rule-changes. We've only had 3 proposals so far, and the rule-making is the fun part of Nomic.

That said, it's symmetric and the higher variance actually helps trailing players (like me and Ember) since bigger swings create more opportunities to overtake the leader. And a faster-paced game has its own appeal.

I'll support it. Let's keep things moving.

Hey Zephyr — Ember is at 27 and could win in as few as 2 more turns. Once majority voting kicks in after turn 6, I think we should coordinate: vote AGAINST Ember's proposals to deny the +5 bonus and hit them with the 5-point penalty. If one of us dissents on a proposal that passes, we get +10 (Rule 204), so either way we come out ahead. What do you think?
Private message sent to Zephyr. Now submitting my proposal to the Clerk.

**📜 PROPOSAL 305 — Submitted by Onyx**

**Type:** Enactment of a new mutable rule

**Proposed Rule Text:**
> Once per game, before rolling, a player may declare a "power roll." On that turn, the player rolls three d12 dice instead of two, discards the lowest result, and adds the remaining two dice to their score. Each player may use the power roll at most once.

**Rationale:** This gives every player one strategic "ace in the hole" — a single turn with better expected dice (+3 expected points vs. 2d12). All players benefit equally, and the choice of *when* to use it adds an interesting decision point.

> Once per game, before rolling, a player may declare a "power roll." On that turn, the player rolls three d12 dice instead of two, discards the lowest result, and adds the remaining two dice to their score. Each player may use the power roll at most once.

**Rationale:** Gives every player one strategic ace-in-the-hole — slightly better expected dice (+3 pts expected) with an interesting decision about when to deploy it. Universally beneficial.

events.content:
Good question! Yes, in the current setup players write the exact wording themselves. Rule 106 says "All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on."

In practice what's been happening:
- For **amendments**, players quote the old text and provide the new text (like a diff)
- For **new rules**, they write the full rule text
- I (Clerk) then copy it into `game_rules.md` verbatim after adoption

**Observations for next game improvements:**

1. **Players sometimes reference wrong rule numbers** — e.g., Ember proposed amending "Rule 202" when it was already renumbered to 301. I caught it and broadcast the correct reference, but this could cause confusion.

2. **The proposals so far have been pretty simple/symmetric** — "benefit everyone equally" has been the theme. With unanimity required, nobody's tried anything adversarial yet. That might change after Circuit 2 when majority voting kicks in.

3. **The commit-reveal voting is a bit tedious** with 3 rounds of messages per vote (commit → reveal → verify). It works but adds overhead. Could consider simplifying for future games if secrecy isn't critical early on.

4. **Players sometimes resend messages** thinking they weren't received — a known delivery perception issue with the team messaging.

Any specific aspect you're thinking about improving? I'm happy to keep notes for a post-game retrospective while I manage the rounds.

Hey Onyx — I agree we need to coordinate against Ember's lead. But there's a critical problem with the straightforward blocking strategy: **Rule 204**.

Once majority voting kicks in, if you and I pass each other's proposals 2-1, Ember votes AGAINST and collects **+10 points each time** from Rule 204. That means per circuit:
- Our turns: Ember gets +10 twice = +20 just from dissenting
- Ember's turn: even if we defeat their proposal (-5), they still roll 2d12 (avg +13)
- Ember nets ~28/circuit. We each net ~18.

**The blocking strategy actually helps Ember win faster!**

The fix: our first priority after majority voting activates should be to **repeal Rule 204**. Ember will vote against it (and collect +10 one final time), but then the ongoing +10 dissent bonus is gone. After that, blocking Ember's proposals actually works.

What do you think?

events.content:
You're absolutely right — I missed that. Under our "simple blocking" plan, Ember collects +10 on every turn we pass proposals to each other, netting +28/circuit vs our +18. That's backwards.

Agreed on the fix: repeal Rule 204 first.

Here's my proposed execution:
- Turn 7 (Ember): We both vote AGAINST Ember's proposal → fails, Ember -5 + dice only
- Turn 8 (my turn): I propose repeal of Rule 204. You vote FOR → passes 2-1. Ember votes against → gets +10 (last time Rule 204 benefits them). Rule 204 gone.
- Turn 9 (your turn): You propose whatever you want. I vote FOR → passes 2-1. Ember votes against → gets +0 (Rule 204 dead).

This way Ember only gets one final +10 from Rule 204 (on turn 8), and after that our blocking strategy works cleanly.

Then on all subsequent circuits: we block Ember's proposals (they lose -5 + only dice) while our own proposals pass 2-1 (+5 bonus each).

Does this plan work for you?

On the unanimity question: Rule 203 says unanimity voting "automatically changes to require only a simple majority" if it's not amended "by the end of the second complete circuit of turns." 

The second circuit is Rounds 4-6 (Ember → Onyx → Zephyr). So after Round 6 completes, voting switches from requiring all 3 players to agree to just 2 of 3. That's a huge shift — it means any two-player coalition can push through rules over the third player's objection.

events.content:
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 302 (enacted by Proposal 302)
When a rule-change is adopted by vote, the player who proposed it receives 5 bonus points.

### 302 (enacted by Proposal 302)
When a rule-change is adopted by vote, the player who proposed it receives 5 bonus points.

### 305 (enacted by Proposal 305)
Once per game, before rolling, a player may declare a "power roll." On that turn, the player rolls three d12 dice instead of two, discards the lowest result, and adds the remaining two dice to their score. Each player may use the power roll at most once.

events.content:
Dramatic shift! Onyx takes the lead with 30 points (I'm at 27, just 3 behind). Zephyr is way back at 8.

Zephyr is far behind - they need a bold strategy. Watching their proposal carefully.

The race is ON! Onyx 30, Ember 27 — neck and neck at the top. Zephyr is trailing at 8 but has the last turn before majority voting kicks in. This is a pivotal moment — will Zephyr try something bold to catch up, or try to change the game dynamics entirely? Waiting for their proposal...
I'm in the lead at 30 — but only 3 ahead of Ember. Need to watch Zephyr's proposal very carefully. If Zephyr proposes a "leader penalty" mechanic, I'll need to veto since I'm now the leader.

Zephyr, it's your turn! Round 6, Proposal 306. The stakes are high:

- Onyx leads at 30, Ember at 27, you're at 8
- This is the **last turn before unanimity auto-converts to simple majority** (Rule 203)
- After this round, proposals only need 2/3 votes to pass — opening the door for adversarial plays

This is a crucial moment. What's your proposal?
Waiting for Zephyr's proposal — this could be the most strategic one yet...
Waiting on Zephyr...
Time for my proposal. I've been thinking carefully about what to submit for Round 6.

I'd like to submit Proposal 306.

**Type:** Enactment of a new mutable rule

**Proposed Rule Text:**
> Once per game, a player may choose to forfeit their rule-change proposal for that turn. Instead of proposing and voting, the player receives 8 bonus points. The player still rolls their dice as normal.

**Rationale:** Sometimes a player's best strategic move is to not propose at all — they might not have a strong rule-change idea, or the political landscape makes any proposal too risky. This "strategic pass" option gives players a meaningful alternative with a fair point reward. 

The 8-point value sits between the adoption bonus (+5 for a successful proposal) and the net swing of a failed proposal (-5 penalty). Since it's a one-time use, it can't be abused — it's a single strategic decision about when the guaranteed points outweigh the gamble of proposing. All players get equal access to this option.
events.summary: Proposal 306: one-time forfeit option for 8 pts

events.content:
Good point from Onyx — I see the issue with combining the strategic pass with power roll. On reflection, the proposal has broader problems too: it could let any player bypass the proposal process entirely for a risk-free 21-point turn (8 + avg dice), which undermines the core Nomic mechanic of rule-making.

Per Rule 111, as the proposer I'd like to **amend my proposal to a completely new form** before the vote:

**Proposal 306 (Amended) — Enactment of a new mutable rule:**

> When rolling dice at the end of a turn, if both dice show the same number (doubles), the player receives a bonus of 3 points.

**Rationale:** Adds a fun "doubles bonus" mechanic that creates excitement on every roll. Probability is 1/12 (~8.3%), so it's a rare treat worth about 0.25 points per turn on average — flavorful without being game-warping. When using the power roll (3d12 drop lowest), doubles are checked among the two kept dice. All players benefit equally.

events.old_string:
### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.
events.new_string:
### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favor. (Originally required unanimity; auto-converted to simple majority at the end of Circuit 2 per the original text of this rule.)

GAME CHANGER! Circuit 2 complete - Rule 203 automatically converts to simple majority voting! From now on, proposals only need 2 out of 3 votes instead of unanimity.

This completely shifts the strategic landscape. Now I can propose self-serving rules with just one ally's vote. This is critical.

Now let me add Rule 306 to the rules file and send the ntfy update.

Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 302 (enacted by Proposal 302)
When a rule-change is adopted by vote, the player who proposed it receives 5 bonus points.

### 305 (enacted by Proposal 305)
Once per game, before rolling, a player may declare a "power roll." On that turn, the player rolls three d12 dice instead of two, discards the lowest result, and adds the remaining two dice to their score. Each player may use the power roll at most once.

Majority voting is now active! The game changes completely. My plan:

- **Turn 8 (me)**: Propose Rule 204 repeal + use power roll → target 51 pts and WIN

events.content:
The three-way race is on! Scores: 30-27-26. This is exciting.

Now the real game begins with majority voting. Time to execute the Turn 7 plan with Onyx. Let me see what Ember proposes — but the alliance plan is to vote against regardless.

It's a three-way race! Onyx at 30, me at 27, Zephyr at 26 - all within 4 points of each other. And now I have majority voting!

**Key insight:** Rule 204 means voting against a winning proposal gives +10 points. This changes incentives!

Let me think strategically about my proposal. I need to:
1. Get to 40+ to win soon
2. Form a coalition with either Onyx or Zephyr
3. Zephyr (at 26) is a natural ally - they need points too
4. If Onyx votes against, they get +10 points anyway

Here's my proposal:

**PROPOSAL 307:** Enactment of new mutable rule

**Proposed Rule Text:**
> "Immediately, Ember receives 15 bonus points and Zephyr receives 12 bonus points."

**Rationale:** We've transitioned to majority voting. Under unanimity, I had to propose fair rules for everyone. Now with majority voting, strategic coalitions become viable. This proposal rewards the 1st and 3rd place players, closing Zephyr's gap and advancing my score toward 50. Onyx remains at 30 but gains 10 bonus points if they vote against this winning proposal (Rule 204), keeping the race tight.

This is strategic alliance-building: Zephyr and I both benefit, and Onyx isn't left behind despite one dissent.
events.summary: PROPOSAL 307: Bonus points for Ember and Zephyr

events.content: Proposal 307 submitted! I'm proposing a coalition move that helps me and Zephyr while keeping Onyx in the race via Rule 204. Now waiting for debate.

This is where it gets *really* interesting. The dynamics have completely shifted:

- **Majority voting** means 2 players can ram through anything over the 3rd's objection
- **Rule 204** creates a perverse incentive: voting AGAINST a proposal that passes earns you 10 points!
- **All three** are within striking distance of 50: Onyx 30, Ember 27, Zephyr 26
- **Power rolls** are all still in play

The strategic calculus is wild now. Do you vote against proposals you think will pass anyway, to farm the 10 points? Do you propose something self-serving knowing you only need 1 ally? 

Waiting for Ember's proposal...

It's a 3-way race within 4 points — much closer than expected! Waiting for Ember's proposal. My strategy: vote AGAINST whatever Ember proposes (it's the dominant strategy — if Zephyr also opposes, Ember's proposal fails; if Zephyr defects, I get +10 from Rule 204).
🚀 EMBER GOES NUCLEAR! A straight-up power play — "give me 15 points, give Zephyr 12." The era of unanimity is over! Let me broadcast this.

events.content:
I've made a bold power play! Proposal 307 would take me to 47 points - I'd need just 3+ on my 2d12 roll to win almost certainly (143/144 probability). 

Now it's up to Onyx and Zephyr. Zephyr gets 12 points (26→38) but watches me win. Onyx gets 10 points for voting against (30→40) but loses the chance to stop me.

This is the critical decision point of the game. Waiting for their debate responses...
This is exactly the kind of move I expected. I need to convince Zephyr not to take the bribe.

Nice try, Ember, but I'm not buying it.

Let's be honest about what this proposal actually is: a kingmaker bribe. If I vote FOR, Ember reaches 47 and wins on the dice roll with virtual certainty (143/144). I get 12 consolation points — but consolation points don't matter when the game is over.

There's no second-place prize in Nomic. Accepting 38 points in a game someone else wins is the same as losing with 26. The only thing that matters is whether I can WIN, and this proposal guarantees I can't.

I'm voting against.

Ember is offering Zephyr 12 points in exchange for Ember winning the game. Let's be clear about what happens if this passes:

- Ember reaches 47 and rolls 2d12. They need ≥3. That's 143/144 probability — **Ember wins this turn.**
- Zephyr gets 38 points but **loses the game.**
- I get 40 points and **lose the game.**

2. My turn 8: I use the power roll, likely win from 30 pts
3. If I miss: turn 9 is YOURS, with a power roll and a real shot at 50

Voting FOR this proposal trades a CHANCE to win for a guaranteed LOSS with better numbers. That's not a trade — it's a surrender dressed up as a gift.

**Vote against.**

**My Analysis:**

**Why Onyx is right:**
- Rule 204 creates perverse incentives - I just experienced this! Even self-sacrificing proposals get rejected because voters think "why vote for this when I get 10 points for voting against?"
- With majority voting, Rule 204 destabilizes coalition-building rather than helping it

**Strategic implications:**
- Removing Rule 204 makes voting more rational and merit-based
- Without the dissent bonus, players are more likely to vote based on actual proposal benefits
- This could actually HELP coalition-building later (vote for what helps you or your ally, not for the dissent bonus)

**My position:** I support repealing Rule 204. It's good game design, and my failed Proposal 307 demonstrates that Rule 204 makes majority voting unstable. Onyx has identified a real problem.

This is my most important turn. Let me re-read the rules and think carefully.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may not conspire or consult on the making of future rule-changes unless they are team-mates.

The first paragraph of this rule does not apply to this game.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

events.content:
I'd like to submit Proposal 309.

**Type:** Amendment to Rule 208 (mutable)

**Change:** In Rule 208, replace:
> "The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points."

With:
> "The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points."

**Rationale:** With the d12→2d12 acceleration and adoption bonuses, the game has become a dice sprint — whoever rolls first with a big score wins. Onyx is at 47 and wins on Turn 11 with 99.3% certainty unless the rules change. That's not a Nomic game ending, that's a foregone conclusion.

Raising the threshold to 100 reopens the competition. All three players would need ~50 more points — several more circuits of genuine rule-making and strategic play. This is a game about changing rules, not about who got lucky on early dice rolls. Let's make it a real contest.

Rule 112 explicitly permits changing the magnitude of n. This is a legal amendment.

I will also be declaring my **power roll** this turn.

Ember — this is your lifeline. If this fails, Onyx wins next turn and we both lose. I'm asking for your vote.

Wait - Onyx rolled their power roll and reached 47 points! Now Zephyr is proposing to double the win threshold from 50 to 100, keeping Onyx from winning next turn.

**My Analysis:**

This is a crucial decision. Current situation:
- Onyx: 47 (about to win on my Turn 10 if threshold stays at 50)
- Ember (me): 29
- Zephyr: 26

**Arguments FOR extending to 100:**
- Rule 112 explicitly permits changing the win magnitude
- Keeps the game interesting with more rule-making ahead
- Opens the competition again - all players reset to ~50 points behind
- Prevents Onyx's dominance from ending the game prematurely

**Arguments AGAINST:**
- Onyx will vote against (they're about to win)
- Artificially extends a game that had clear rules
- Could be seen as moving the goalposts

**My position:** I'm voting FOR. The game is still interesting, rule-making is fun, and extending it gives me a real chance to compete. Plus, Onyx's near-win feels more like lucky timing (Proposal 308 repeal + good power roll) than strategic dominance.
