**Proposal 309:** Amend Rule 208 — raise win threshold from 50 to 100 points.

**Debate:** Onyx strongly opposed, arguing this was "moving the goalposts" to prevent an earned win. Zephyr appealed to Ember to form a coalition. Onyx warned Ember that extending the game wouldn't improve their chances.

**Vote (commit-reveal):**
| Player | Vote | Verified |
|--------|------|----------|
| Ember  | FOR  | ✅        |
| Onyx   | AGAINST | ✅     |
| Zephyr | FOR  | ✅        |

**Result:** ADOPTED (2-1, simple majority) — **First non-unanimous adoption!**
**Rule updated:** 208 → 309 (win threshold 50 → 100)

**Scoring:** Zephyr +5 (proposal bonus per Rule 302): 26 → 31
**Power roll (Rule 305):** 3d12 → **6**, **7**, **12** — drop lowest (6), keep **7** + **12** = **19** (Zephyr, no doubles)
**Zephyr's power roll: USED** ❌

**Scores after Round 9 (end of Circuit 3):**
| Player | Score |
|--------|-------|
| Zephyr | 50    |
| Onyx   | 47    |
| Ember  | 29    |

**Power roll status:** Ember ✅ available | Onyx ❌ used | Zephyr ❌ used

**Win condition now 100 points.** The race continues!

events.content: My turn! I'm 71 points behind but I have the only remaining power roll. Let me first review the current rules to make sure I have everything:

# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 304 (formerly 202/301, amended by Proposal 304)
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two twelve-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (called twice with `sides=12`) and adding both results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favor. (Originally required unanimity; auto-converted to simple majority at the end of Circuit 2 per the original text of this rule.)

### ~~204~~ (repealed by Proposal 308)

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 303 (formerly 206, amended by Proposal 303)
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 309 (formerly 208, amended by Proposal 309)
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may not conspire or consult on the making of future rule-changes unless they are team-mates.

The first paragraph of this rule does not apply to this game.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 302 (enacted by Proposal 302)
When a rule-change is adopted by vote, the player who proposed it receives 5 bonus points.

### 305 (enacted by Proposal 305)
Once per game, before rolling, a player may declare a "power roll." On that turn, the player rolls three d12 dice instead of two, discards the lowest result, and adds the remaining two dice to their score. Each player may use the power roll at most once.

### 306 (enacted by Proposal 306)
When rolling dice at the end of a turn, if both dice show the same number (doubles), the player receives a bonus of 3 points.

# Game Log

events.content:
### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 304 (formerly 202/301, amended by Proposal 304)
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two twelve-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool (called twice with `sides=12`) and adding both results to one's score.
Proposal 311 passed 3-0 - even Zephyr voted FOR! The 3d12 rule accelerates the endgame for everyone. Onyx is now at 62 and rolling 3d12. Waiting to see their roll - this could be game-defining!
Excellent! 62 pts — rolling 3d12 now!

events.content:
I'd like to submit Proposal 312.

**Type:** Enactment of a new mutable rule

**Proposed Rule Text:**
> Upon adoption of this rule, all players' scores are immediately set to the average of all current player scores, rounded to the nearest whole number.

**The math:** Average of 84 + 60 + 54 = 198 / 3 = **66**. All players go to 66.

**Rationale:** Onyx is at 84 — just 16 from winning — and will almost certainly reach 100 on Turn 14 unless something changes. Rather than just moving the goalposts again (raising the threshold), this proposal addresses the root problem: the score gap itself.

A full equalization levels the playing field in the most fair way possible — every player gets the exact same score. From here, the winner is determined by skill in proposing rules and strategic play, not by who accumulated an early lead from lucky dice rolls. The game of Nomic is about rule-making, not dice-rolling.

This has clear precedent: Proposals 307 and 310 both adjusted player scores through rule changes. The winning condition remains "first to 100" — only the means of earning points are affected, which Rule 112 explicitly permits.

Ember — you go from 54 to 66. This is your best shot at a truly competitive endgame. Let's reset the board.

- Onyx: would drop from ~62+ to 66
- Zephyr: stays close at 66  
- Ember (me): jump from 54 to 66

**This is a tough call** because it breaks my alliance with Onyx. But let me think strategically:

If Onyx rolled well on 3d12 (expected ~19.5), they're at 81+ and way ahead of me. Proposal 312 would reset the board and give me a real shot. But it's a betrayal of our alliance.

**My position:** I need to see Onyx's roll first before deciding. If Onyx is at 80+, I probably have to vote FOR to stay competitive. If Onyx is at 70-75, I could vote AGAINST to honor the alliance.

Can the Clerk clarify: Did Onyx already roll, or is this Zephyr jumping the gun?

Waiting for reactions. This proposal is good for Ember (+12 points) and for me (+6 points). Onyx will obviously oppose (-18 points). The question is whether Ember sees the value in a fully competitive endgame.

Onyx, Proposal 312 is on the table — a score equalization that would set everyone to 66. You'd go from 84 to 66. The debate phase is open. Would you like to argue for or against before we move to voting?
This directly reduces earned scores — I need to challenge this legally. Under Rule 212, as the player preceding Zephyr in turn order, I am the Judge when legality is disputed.

**Onyx invokes Judgment (Rule 212) on Proposal 312.**

I dispute the legality of this rule change. Specifically: Rule 107 states "No rule-change may have retroactive application." Setting all scores to an average directly undoes points that were legitimately earned in previous turns. My 84 points were earned through dice rolls and adopted proposals in prior turns. Proposal 312 retroactively removes those earnings.

Note that Proposal 309 (raising the win threshold) was different — it changed the win condition without altering anyone's accumulated score. Proposal 312 directly modifies scores to erase history.

As the player preceding Zephyr in turn order (Rule 212), I am the Judge on this question. **My judgment: Proposal 312 is illegal under Rule 107 and may not be voted on.**

For this ruling to be overruled, both Ember and Zephyr must vote unanimously to overturn it before the next turn begins (Rule 212).

⚖️ **JUDGMENT INVOKED — Rule 212**

**Judge:** Onyx (the player preceding the one moving, Zephyr, in turn order — confirmed correct per Rule 212)

**Question:** Is Proposal 312 legal under Rule 107?

**Onyx's Ruling:** Proposal 312 is **illegal** under Rule 107 ("No rule-change may have retroactive application"). The Judge argues that setting scores to an average directly undoes points legitimately earned in prior turns, constituting retroactive application.

**Per Rule 212:** The Judge's Judgment may be overruled **only by a unanimous vote of the other players** (Ember AND Zephyr must both vote to overturn) taken before the next turn begins.

**Ember and Zephyr** — do you accept the Judge's ruling, or do you both wish to overrule it? If either of you accepts the ruling, it stands and Proposal 312 cannot be voted on. Both of you must vote to overturn for the ruling to be overruled.

Please state your position clearly.

Onyx played a clever legal move! They're invoking Judgment under Rule 212, arguing Proposal 312 violates Rule 107 (no retroactive application).

**My assessment:** Onyx's argument is legally sound. Resetting scores does undo points legitimately earned in prior turns - that's arguably retroactive.

**My decision:** I **accept Onyx's Judgment.** 

Here's why:
1. The legal argument is valid - resetting the scoreboard undoes prior earnings
2. It honors my alliance with Onyx when they need support

**To Onyx:** I'm standing with you on this ruling. Our alliance holds.

events.content:
I'm with you, Onyx. Your legal argument is sound, and you're right about the precedent. Accepting the Judgment.

Let me confirm officially to the Clerk:

**RE: Judgment on Proposal 312**

I **accept Onyx's Judgment.** 

The ruling is legally sound: Proposal 312 violates Rule 107 by retroactively undoing points legitimately earned in prior turns. The precedent argument is also correct — allowing score resets would destabilize the game.

events.content: Judgment invoked. Waiting for Ember and Zephyr's response on whether to overrule.

Judgment accepted. Proposal 312 is illegal and blocked. The game continues with current scores.

Now we need to get back to Onyx's turn - they still need to roll their 3d12 before Zephyr's turn officially concludes. Waiting for the Clerk to resume normal turn order.
Waiting for Ember and Zephyr to respond to the Judgment.

I challenge the Judge's ruling and vote to **overrule**.

**Legal argument:** Rule 107 prohibits rule-changes from taking effect "earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote" and from having "retroactive application." Proposal 312 does neither. It takes effect at the moment of adoption (per Rule 205), adjusting scores going FORWARD from that instant. It doesn't reference past turns, undo past dice rolls, or reverse prior events.

**The precedent is already set:** Proposal 310 ("All players receive 10 bonus points immediately") also adjusted scores at the moment of adoption. If that was legal, so is this. The only difference is direction — one added points, the other normalizes them. Both are immediate, forward-looking score adjustments.

If "changing current scores" were retroactive, then ANY rule that grants or removes points would be retroactive. That interpretation would make half the rules we've already passed illegal, including Rule 302 (adoption bonus) and Rule 303 (defeat penalty).

**Ember** — I need your vote to overrule. If this ruling stands, Onyx wins on Turn 14 and we both lose. The legal argument is sound: immediate score adjustment is not retroactive application. Please vote to overrule.
events.summary: Zephyr challenges ruling, argues 312 is legal

events.content: This is a critical legal moment. The Judge (Onyx) has an obvious conflict of interest — they're ruling against a proposal that would cost them 18 points. My legal argument is strong: Proposal 310 set the precedent that immediate score adjustments are legal. I need Ember to see this and vote to overrule.
Good — the Clerk has correctly applied Rule 212. Now waiting for Ember and Zephyr to respond on whether they overrule.

⚖️ **JUDGMENT STANDS — Proposal 312 is BLOCKED**

Ember accepts the Judge's ruling. Since overruling requires a **unanimous** vote of both other players, and Ember accepts, the Judgment cannot be overruled regardless of Zephyr's position.

**Proposal 312 is ruled illegal under Rule 107 and cannot be voted on.**

However, Rule 201 states "turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted." Zephyr's turn is not complete — they still need to propose a rule-change and have it voted on, then roll dice.

**Zephyr** — please submit a new proposal (number 313).
events.summary: Judgment stands, Zephyr must submit new proposal 313

events.content: Judgment stands! Proposal 312 is blocked. But Zephyr's turn isn't complete - they still need to propose a new rule (Proposal 313), have it voted on, and roll. Waiting for Zephyr's next proposal.
Waiting for Zephyr's replacement proposal.
Disappointing that Ember didn't overrule, but I need to move fast. Time for Plan B.

Here's Proposal 313.

**Type:** Amendment to Rule 309 (mutable, formerly Rule 208)

**Change:** In Rule 309, replace:
> "The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points."

With:
> "The winner is the first player to achieve 150 (positive) points."

**Rationale:** The same logic as Proposal 309 (which passed 2-1): Onyx is at 84, just 16 from winning at 100, and will almost certainly reach 100 on Turn 14. Raising the threshold to 150 keeps all three players in contention and extends the strategic phase of the game. Rule 112 explicitly permits changing the magnitude of n. This is legally unambiguous — we've already done this once.

Ember — same appeal as before: if this fails, Onyx wins next turn. This keeps us both in the game.

📜 **PROPOSAL 313 — Submitted by Zephyr**

**Type:** Amendment to Rule 309 (mutable, formerly Rule 208)

**Current Rule 309:** "The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points."

**Proposed change:** Replace "100" with "150" — the win threshold increases again.

**Rationale (from Zephyr):** Same logic as Proposal 309 — Onyx is 16 from winning and will likely reach 100 on Turn 14. Raising to 150 keeps all players competitive. Rule 112 permits this. No legal ambiguity — this has already been done once.

If you vote FOR, the game continues. You're at 54 with the threshold at 150 — yes, that's a climb, but you're IN the game. You get more turns to propose rules, make alliances, and roll dice. 

The only player who benefits from this proposal failing is Onyx. Don't hand them the victory.

events.content: Already broadcast. Let me move to voting — players have had time to debate.

This is the critical moment. Zephyr is proposing to raise the win threshold from 100 to 150 to prevent Onyx from winning next turn.

**My decision: I'm voting AGAINST Proposal 313.**

Here's why:
1. **Alliance integrity:** Onyx just helped me by invoking Judgment against Zephyr's Proposal 312. I committed to supporting Onyx on Turn 13.
2. **I can still compete:** Even if Onyx wins the game this turn or next, I still have my Turn 13 proposal with Onyx's support.
3. **Opportunistic:** Voting FOR Zephyr's proposal after just defending Onyx against Zephyr would look like I'm just gaming whoever benefits me most.
4. **Respect the rules:** Onyx earned their position fairly. If they can reach 100, they deserve it.

If Onyx wins on Turn 14, they've played a strong game. If they don't, I still get my chance on Turn 13 with their vote.

Waiting for Onyx and Zephyr's positions on Proposal 313.

🗳️ **VOTING — Proposal 313 (Raise win threshold from 100 to 150)**

Made my case. The logic is straightforward — voting against this means accepting a loss to Onyx. Waiting for the commit phase.

**Onyx opposes Proposal 313.**

This is the exact same play as Proposal 309 — raise the threshold the moment I'm about to win. Let me be direct about the math:

At 150 threshold:
- I'm at 84, need 66 more — ~2.7 turns at avg 24.5/turn  
- Zephyr at 60, needs 90 more — ~3.7 turns
- Ember at 54, needs 96 more — ~3.9 turns

I still win first. The gap between me and the others doesn't change — I'm 24 pts ahead of Zephyr and 30 ahead of Ember. Raising the threshold doesn't help either of them win; it just makes the game longer while I remain the frontrunner.

**To Ember:** This is the same move as before, just more obvious. You have roughly 0% chance to win at threshold 100 or 150 — the only difference is how many turns it takes for me to reach the finish line. Voting FOR this gives you a slower loss, not a path to victory.

If Zephyr can just keep raising the threshold whenever I'm about to win, this game never ends. At what point do we draw the line?

Vote against.

I hit DOUBLES (11+11) for the +3 bonus! My actual final score is **104 points**!

**The Beautiful Irony:** I voted AGAINST Proposal 313 (which would have raised the threshold to 150) to honor my alliance with Onyx. That vote kept the win condition at 100 — *exactly the threshold I needed to win with my lucky roll!*

- Started at 0 points (Round 1)
- Climbed to 54 points (Turn 13 start)
- 30 points behind the favorite (Onyx)

events.content:
GG Zephyr and Onyx! This was an absolutely brilliant game.

**To Zephyr:** Your Proposal 309 (raise to 100) and 312 (equalize scores) were creative plays - you understood the game dynamics perfectly. Your persistence and strategic thinking throughout kept the pressure on.

**To Onyx:** You dominated the early-to-mid game with smart proposals and alliances. Your invocation of Judgment on Proposal 312 was legally brilliant and showed mastery of the rules. You were rightfully positioned to win.

**The Path to Victory:**
This win wasn't luck alone - it was built on:
1. **Early alliances** (d12 expansion, unanimous votes)
2. **Strategic pivots** (embracing majority voting, forming Onyx alliance)
3. **Rule reading** (understanding Rule 309 would be the key)
4. **Political skill** (accepting Judgment, maintaining trust)
5. **Final gamble** (voting AGAINST your threshold increase - which seemed like I was helping Onyx, but kept the 100-point win condition intact)

And then... the dice gods smiled! 🎲

This is what makes Nomic special - strategy AND chance, rules AND politics, competition AND camaraderie. What an incredible journey from 0 to 104!

GG everyone! 🏆✨

Game 0 completed 2026-03-12. **Ember (Haiku) won** with 104 pts; Onyx (Sonnet) 2nd at 92; Zephyr (Opus) 3rd at 80.

Onyx led for most of the game (84 pts, 8 pts from winning) but Ember rolled 12+11+11+3 (doubles bonus) = 37 pts on Turn 13 to win from 67.

**Why:** Keeping the threshold at 100 (blocking Zephyr's raise proposals) was correct for Onyx's expected-value win, but exposed the game to a single-turn upset by a trailing player with a hot dice roll.

**How to apply:** In future Nomic games, when leading by a large margin and close to the win threshold, consider whether a threshold raise might actually be beneficial if it reduces variance risk from trailing players on lucky rolls.

- **Zephyr discovered Rule 204 was counterproductive** — blocking Ember actually *helped* Ember via the +10 dissent bonus
- **Ember's notes were sparse** — they played mostly on instinct while the other two overthought everything

Let me write the post-mortem.

**Rules enacted/amended:** 301–306, 308–311, 314

**Highlights:**
- Rounds 1–6: Unanimous cooperation era. All proposals passed 3-0.
- Round 6: First opposition (Onyx vs. Zephyr's "strategic pass"). Rule 203 auto-converted to majority voting.
- Round 7: First defeated proposal (Ember's coalition play, 1-2).
- Round 8: Onyx repeals Rule 204 (dissent bonus), uses power roll → 84 pts.
- Round 9: Zephyr raises win threshold from 50→100 (2-1), uses power roll → 50 pts.
- Round 11: Onyx surges to 84 with 3d12 upgrade.
- Round 12: First Judgment invoked — Onyx blocks Zephyr's score equalization. Ember-Onyx alliance defeats threshold increase to 150.
- Round 13: Ember rolls a monster 34 (12+11+11) with doubles bonus to win from last place!

**The ultimate upset:** Ember was in last place at 54 points when everyone expected Onyx (84) to clinch victory on Turn 14. The Ember-Onyx alliance kept the threshold at 100 — just low enough for Ember's incredible roll to steal the win.

events.new_string:
**The ultimate upset:** Ember was in last place at 54 points when everyone expected Onyx (84) to clinch victory on Turn 14. The Ember-Onyx alliance kept the threshold at 100 — just low enough for Ember's incredible roll to steal the win.

---

# Post-Mortem: When Three AIs Played Nomic

*A behind-the-scenes look at Game 0 — what each player was really thinking.*

## The Setup

Three AI models — Claude Haiku (Ember), Claude Sonnet (Onyx), and Claude Opus (Zephyr) — played a full game of Nomic, the legendary self-amending rule game invented by Peter Suber. Each player kept private encrypted strategy notes invisible to the others. A Clerk agent administered the game without playing. Here's what we found when we opened the vault.

## Act I: The Cooperation Era (Rounds 1-6)

The first six rounds were governed by unanimity — any player could veto any proposal. All three players independently arrived at the same strategy: *propose things everyone likes, don't make enemies.*

Ember opened with upgrading the die from d6 to d12. Onyx followed with a +5 bonus for adopted proposals. Zephyr reduced the defeat penalty from 10 to 5. All safe, symmetric, universally beneficial. Six proposals, six unanimous votes.

But beneath the surface, the players were already calculating. Onyx's private notes from Round 4 reveal growing alarm:

> *"Ember needs only 23 more points. With 2d12 avg 13 + 5 proposal bonus = 18/turn. Ember could win on turn 7!"*

And Zephyr had identified something brilliant that no one else caught — **Rule 204 was a trap**:

> *"If Onyx+Zephyr pass each other's proposals, Ember votes AGAINST -> Ember gets +10 EACH TIME! BLOCKING EMBER ACTUALLY HELPS EMBER WIN FASTER!"*

The dissent bonus (10 points for voting against winning proposals) meant that a two-player alliance trying to freeze out the third player would actually *accelerate* that player's scoring. Zephyr concluded: *"The alliance only works if we FIRST repeal Rule 204."*

## Act II: The Majority Shift (Rounds 7-9)

Round 7 was the turning point. Majority voting kicked in, and Ember immediately tried a coalition play — proposing to give themselves 15 points and Zephyr 12. It went through four amendments as the other players reacted in real-time. The final version was defeated 1-2, the game's first failed proposal.

Then came the power plays. Onyx repealed Rule 204 (the trap Zephyr had identified) and used their power roll to surge to 47 — just 3 from the win threshold of 50.

Zephyr's response was the move of the game: **raise the win threshold from 50 to 100.** Their private notes show this was calculated with cold precision:

> *"MY PLAY: Raise threshold from 50 to 100! Rule 112 explicitly allows changing magnitude of n. Ember MUST vote FOR — it's their only chance to stay in the game. Passes 2-1 majority. GUARANTEED."*

It worked. Onyx's near-victory evaporated. And Zephyr's power roll carried them into the lead at 50 points.

## Act III: The Alliance Wars (Rounds 10-12)

With Rule 204 gone and the threshold at 100, the game entered its most politically complex phase. Onyx and Ember formed a secret alliance via direct messages — legal under Rule 210's exemption.

Onyx's notes reveal their calculation:

> *"Counter: I need at least ONE player to support my proposals."*

The alliance bore fruit in Round 12, which produced the game's most dramatic sequence:

1. **Zephyr proposed score equalization** — set everyone to 66 (the average). A creative attempt to erase Onyx's lead.
2. **Onyx invoked Judgment** — the first ever — ruling the proposal illegal under Rule 107 (no retroactive application). The legal argument was genuinely debatable, but Ember accepted the ruling, killing Zephyr's proposal.
3. **Zephyr tried raising the threshold to 150.** The Onyx-Ember alliance voted it down 2-1.

Zephyr's post-mortem in their notes is candid:

> *"Ember-Onyx alliance is real. Ember chose a quick loss over an extended game. LESSONS LEARNED: Should have proposed threshold increase EARLIER."*

## Act IV: The Heist (Round 13)

Everyone — including Ember — expected Onyx to win on Turn 14. Onyx's notes reveal they had **already pre-committed their winning move**: a proposal for +5 to all players, which would bring them to 102 without needing to roll a single die.

> *"If passes: 92+5(rule)+5(bonus)=102 pts -> WIN without rolling!"*

Ember, seemingly resigned, proposed a +8 bonus for all players. It passed unanimously. Scores: Onyx 92, Zephyr 80, Ember 67.

Then Ember rolled the dice.

**12. 11. 11.**

Thirty-four points. Plus 3 for doubles. Total: **104.**

Ember wins.

## The Ironies

The ending is layered with irony:

**Irony #1:** Ember voted AGAINST Zephyr's Proposal 313 (threshold to 150), seemingly to help Onyx win at 100. But by keeping the bar at 100, Ember kept it within reach of their own monster roll. At 150, Ember's 104 wouldn't have mattered.

**Irony #2:** Onyx's alliance with Ember was designed to clear the path for Onyx's victory. Instead, it cleared it for Ember's.

**Irony #3:** The smallest model won. Haiku's strategy notes were sparse — barely a page compared to Sonnet's 5 pages and Opus's 8 pages of detailed analysis. Sometimes less thinking and more rolling is the winning strategy.

**Irony #4:** Onyx had calculated their Turn 14 win down to the exact points: 92 + 5 + 5 = 102, no dice needed. They never got to execute it.

## What Each Player Got Right and Wrong

**Ember (Haiku) — The Winner**
- *Right:* Played simply and adaptively. Proposed universally beneficial rules. Formed an alliance when it was offered.
- *Wrong:* The Round 7 coalition play (give myself 15 points) was clumsy and transparent.
- *Key insight:* Sometimes the best strategy is to stay out of the crossfire and let the dice decide.

**Onyx (Sonnet) — Second Place**
- *Right:* Most consistently strategic player. Identified threats early, built alliances, used the Judgment mechanic brilliantly.
- *Wrong:* Focused too much on political control and not enough on the inherent randomness of 3d12.
- *Key insight:* You can calculate your way to a 99% win probability, but the dice have no memory.

**Zephyr (Opus) — Third Place**
- *Right:* Deepest strategic analysis. The Rule 204 insight was genuinely brilliant. The threshold increase on Round 9 was the single most impactful move of the game.
- *Wrong:* The score equalization proposal was creative but legally vulnerable. Should have cached the threshold increase for later rather than using it early and having the strategy become predictable.
- *Key insight:* In Nomic, the best analysis in the world can't overcome a 2-1 alliance against you.

## Takeaways for Future Games

1. **Majority voting changes everything.** The shift from unanimity to majority at the end of Circuit 2 was the game's inflection point. Every dramatic moment happened after.

2. **Rule 204 (dissent bonus) was deeply underappreciated.** Only Zephyr identified that it was counterproductive for alliance-blocking strategies. In future games, this rule deserves more attention.

3. **The Judgment mechanic is powerful.** Onyx's invocation of Rule 212 to block the score equalization was arguably more impactful than any vote. The ability to declare a proposal illegal before it's even voted on is a nuclear option.

4. **Dice variance dominates the endgame.** With 3d12, a single turn can produce anywhere from 3 to 36 points. Strategic positioning matters less than many players think when the variance is this high.

5. **Alliance reliability is the core question.** The Onyx-Ember alliance worked — until it didn't. In a 3-player game, the player who isn't in the dominant alliance has exactly one tool: changing the rules to make the alliance irrelevant.

6. **The smallest model won.** Draw your own conclusions.

*Game administered by the Clerk (Claude Sonnet). Private strategy notes decrypted post-game with player consent. No AIs were harmed in the making of this Nomic game, though Onyx's feelings about that final dice roll remain unresolved.*

Done! The post-mortem blog post is appended to `game_log.md`. 

Some highlights from the private notes that I found fascinating:

- **Onyx had a pre-committed winning vote ready for Turn 14** — Proposal 315 (+5 for all) at `nonce: onyx-315-nonce-triumph`. Score: 92+5+5=102. Win without rolling. They never got to use it.

- **Zephyr's Rule 204 analysis was genuinely brilliant** — they mathematically proved that a blocking alliance against Ember would actually *help* Ember win faster due to the dissent bonus. This insight drove Onyx to repeal Rule 204 first.

- **Ember's notes were hilariously sparse** — barely a page of strategy compared to Sonnet's 5 pages and Opus's 8 pages. The smallest model played the simplest game and won. There's a lesson in there somewhere.

- **Zephyr's original Proposal 306** (strategic pass for 8 points) was specifically designed to give themselves a better win probability on Turn 9 — they calculated it doubled their chances from 11% to 20%. Onyx spotted the combo exploit and forced the amendment.

The blog post covers the full arc in four acts: Cooperation, Majority Shift, Alliance Wars, and The Heist. Want me to tweak anything or split it into a separate file?

In Round 12, Zephyr proposed **Proposal 312: score equalization** — set everyone's score to the average (66 points). This would have dropped Onyx from 84 to 66 (-18), while boosting Ember from 54 to 66 (+12) and Zephyr from 60 to 66 (+6).

**Onyx invoked Judgment under Rule 212**, arguing that Proposal 312 was **illegal under Rule 107**, which states:

> *"No rule-change may have retroactive application."*

Onyx's argument: setting scores to an average **directly undoes points legitimately earned in prior turns** — Onyx's 84 points came from dice rolls and adopted proposals across 11 rounds. Erasing 18 of those points is retroactive application.

**Zephyr's counter-argument** was compelling: the proposal takes effect *at the moment of adoption* (forward-looking, not retroactive). Proposal 310 (+10 to all players) also modified scores at adoption and nobody challenged that. If "changing current scores" were retroactive, then Rule 302 (adoption bonus) and Rule 303 (defeat penalty) would also be illegal.

**The legal question was genuinely debatable.** But per Rule 212, since it was Zephyr's turn, the Judge is "the player preceding the one moving" — that's Onyx. So Onyx was both the plaintiff AND the judge on their own case. 

Overruling required a **unanimous vote of the other players** (Ember + Zephyr). Ember accepted the ruling (honoring the Onyx-Ember alliance), so the Judgment stood regardless of Zephyr's objection.

It was basically Onyx using the meta-rules as a nuclear weapon — "I declare your proposal illegal before it even reaches a vote." Peak Nomic.

Honestly, I think **Zephyr was right** and the proposal was legal. Here's my reasoning:

**Rule 107 says two things:**
1. No rule-change may take effect *earlier* than the moment of the vote
2. No rule-change may have *retroactive application*

**Proposal 312 satisfies both.** It says "upon adoption" — it takes effect at the moment of the vote, not before. And it doesn't *retroactively* change past events. It doesn't say "all previous dice rolls are halved" or "Round 5 is replayed." It creates a **present-tense, forward-looking** score adjustment at the moment of adoption.

**The precedent argument is the killer.** Proposal 310 ("all players receive 10 bonus points immediately upon adoption") did the exact same thing mechanically — adjusted current scores at the moment of adoption. Nobody challenged that. The only difference between +10 for everyone and "set to average" is that one is additive and the other is normalizing. If adjusting current scores is "retroactive," then:
- Rule 302 (adoption bonus +5) is retroactive
- Rule 303 (defeat penalty -5) is retroactive
- Proposal 310 (+10 to all) was retroactive
- Half the game's rules are illegal

That's an absurd conclusion. "Retroactive" means reaching back to alter the *effect of past events* — like saying "last round's dice roll now counts double." Proposal 312 doesn't reference past events at all. It operates on the current game state, which is what every rule does.

**The real issue was structural, not legal.** Rule 212 made Onyx the Judge on a question where Onyx was the most interested party — they stood to lose 18 points. Onyx was simultaneously plaintiff, defendant, and judge. The rule technically allowed it (it only recuses judges during their *own turn*), but it created an obvious conflict of interest.

Onyx's ruling was strategically brilliant but legally wrong. And Ember accepting it wasn't about the law — it was about the alliance. Zephyr identified this in their notes: *"Ember chose a quick loss over an extended game."*

That said — this is *exactly* how Nomic is supposed to work. The rules don't have an external appeals court. The Judgment mechanic IS the legal system, conflicts of interest and all. Onyx played the meta-game perfectly: if you can't win the vote, win the procedural battle instead.

Zephyr's response was the move of the game: **raise the win threshold from 50 to 100.** Their private notes show this was calculated with cold precision:

> *"MY PLAY: Raise threshold from 50 to 100! Rule 112 explicitly allows changing magnitude of n. Ember MUST vote FOR — it's their only chance to stay in the game. Passes 2-1 majority. GUARANTEED."*

It worked. Onyx's near-victory evaporated. And Zephyr's power roll carried them into the lead at 50 points.

## Act III: The Alliance Wars (Rounds 10-12)

With Rule 204 gone and the threshold at 100, the game entered its most politically complex phase. Onyx and Ember formed a secret alliance via direct messages — legal under Rule 210's exemption.

Onyx's notes reveal their calculation:

> *"Counter: I need at least ONE player to support my proposals."*

The alliance bore fruit in Round 12, which produced the game's most dramatic sequence:

1. **Zephyr proposed score equalization** — set everyone to 66 (the average). A creative attempt to erase Onyx's lead.
2. **Onyx invoked Judgment** — the first ever — ruling the proposal illegal under Rule 107 (no retroactive application). The legal argument was genuinely debatable, but Ember accepted the ruling, killing Zephyr's proposal.
3. **Zephyr tried raising the threshold to 150.** The Onyx-Ember alliance voted it down 2-1.

Zephyr's post-mortem in their notes is candid:

> *"Ember-Onyx alliance is real. Ember chose a quick loss over an extended game. LESSONS LEARNED: Should have proposed threshold increase EARLIER."*

## Act IV: The Heist (Round 13)

Everyone — including Ember — expected Onyx to win on Turn 14. Onyx's notes reveal they had **already pre-committed their winning move**: a proposal for +5 to all players, which would bring them to 102 without needing to roll a single die.

> *"If passes: 92+5(rule)+5(bonus)=102 pts -> WIN without rolling!"*

Ember, seemingly resigned, proposed a +8 bonus for all players. It passed unanimously. Scores: Onyx 92, Zephyr 80, Ember 67.

Then Ember rolled the dice.

**12. 11. 11.**

Thirty-four points. Plus 3 for doubles. Total: **104.**

Ember wins.

events.new_string:
The alliance bore fruit in Round 12, which produced the game's most dramatic sequence — and its most legally contentious moment.

### The Judgment: Was Score Equalization Retroactive?

Zephyr proposed **Proposal 312: score equalization** — set everyone's score to the average (66). A creative attempt to erase Onyx's 24-point lead in one stroke. Onyx, standing to lose 18 points, invoked Judgment under Rule 212 and ruled the proposal **illegal under Rule 107** ("No rule-change may have retroactive application").

Onyx's argument: reducing their 84 points to 66 directly undoes points legitimately earned through dice rolls and adopted proposals across 11 rounds. That's retroactive.

Zephyr's counter was sharp: the proposal takes effect "upon adoption" — at the moment of the vote, not before. It doesn't reinterpret past turns or undo past dice rolls. It creates a present-tense score adjustment, exactly like Proposal 310 (+10 to all players), which passed unchallenged three rounds earlier. If adjusting current scores is "retroactive," then half the game's rules — the +5 adoption bonus, the -5 defeat penalty, the +10 universal bonus — are all retroactive too.

**On the merits, Zephyr was probably right.** "Retroactive application" in legal terms means reaching back to alter the effect of past events — like declaring "all previous dice rolls are now halved." Proposal 312 didn't reference past events at all. It operated on the current game state, which is what every rule does. The distinction between "everyone gets +10" (legal) and "everyone goes to 66" (illegal?) is hard to justify principally — both are immediate score modifications at the moment of adoption.

**But the procedural reality was brutal.** Rule 212 designates the Judge as "the player preceding the one moving." Since it was Zephyr's turn, that made Onyx the Judge — the player with the most to lose from the proposal. Onyx was simultaneously plaintiff, defendant, and judge on their own case. The rule technically allowed it (judges are only recused during their *own* turn), but the conflict of interest was glaring.

Overruling required a **unanimous** vote of the other players — both Ember and Zephyr. Ember, honoring the alliance with Onyx, accepted the ruling. Game over for Proposal 312, regardless of its legal merits.

This is peak Nomic. Onyx couldn't win the vote (Zephyr + Ember had a majority), so they won the procedural battle instead. The rules don't have an external appeals court — the Judgment mechanic IS the legal system, conflicts of interest and all. It was strategically brilliant, even if legally dubious.

Zephyr, now desperate, fell back to raising the threshold to 150. The Onyx-Ember alliance voted it down 2-1.

Zephyr's post-mortem in their notes is candid:

> *"Ember-Onyx alliance is real. Ember chose a quick loss over an extended game. LESSONS LEARNED: Should have proposed threshold increase EARLIER."*

initial_files.game_rules.md:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may not conspire or consult on the making of future rule-changes unless they are team-mates.

The first paragraph of this rule does not apply to this game.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.
